docs.title: Welcome to KONJAM
docs.text:
Vanakkam! Welcome to KONJAM (Knowledge Of Native Jargon And Madras — and yes, we totally forced that backronym 🙃).

🏆 Your Chennai Journey 🌴 Tourist

Just arrived at Central. Confused but curious.

0% to next rank Suffix Station --- Rickshaw Respect --- Adjust-Paani ---

docs.title: 🎯 Our Mission
docs.text:
Our goal is to provide a phonetic-only, Chennai-functional Tamil learning resource. This guide is built specifically for non-Tamil speakers in Chennai—such as IT professionals, recent migrants, and long-term residents—who want to survive and thrive through spoken language without needing to learn the formal written script.

We focus on maximizing your return on investment by teaching you high-frequency vocabulary and practical phrases that you can use immediately in your daily life in Namma Chennai.

docs.title: 👥 Target Audience
docs.text:
This guide is for you if:

  • You live in Chennai and want to interact confidently with locals, auto drivers, shopkeepers, and colleagues.
  • You want to learn practical, street-smart Tamil (the Chennai dialect, like Saptiya? instead of the textbook Saapiteergala?).
  • You find learning a new script intimidating and prefer to learn through transliteration (reading Tamil words in English letters).
  • You relate well to structural bridges, which we use to make grammar easier to grasp intuitively.

docs.title: 📖 Course Preview
docs.text:
Here is a sneak peek at what you will learn in the upcoming modules:

  • 1. History & Nuances: Understand the cultural context, the Chennai dialect, and how this guide approaches learning.
  • 2. Survival & Emergencies: Essential phrases for getting out of tricky situations, asking for help, and basic navigation.
  • 3. Social Interactions: Greetings, making friends, and polite conversation.
  • 4. Time & Numbers: Telling time, bargaining, and handling money effectively.
  • 5. Verbs & Grammar: The engine of the language—how to put sentences together using practical grammatical bridges.
  • 6. Adjectives & Limits: Describing things, people, and situations properly.
  • 7. Slang & Tanglish: Sounding like a true Chennaite with local colloquialisms.
  • 8. Travel & Conflict: Navigating autos, buses, MRTS, and successfully resolving disputes without escalating.
  • 9. Everyday Logistics & Services: Dealing with delivery drivers, landlords, basic repairs, and office navigation.
  • 10. Household, Groceries & Nature: Interacting with domestic help, identifying local vegetables, and handling daily errands.

Dive in, practice speaking out loud, and don't worry about being perfect. Konjam Konjam (little by little), you'll get there!

docs.title: 1. History & Nuances
docs.text:
Welcome to your first lesson in Tamil!

Tamil is one of the longest-surviving classical languages in the world. Learning just a little bit of Tamil—konjam Tamil—will go a long way in navigating Chennai.

docs.title: Pronunciation Basics
docs.text: Tamil pronunciation has some unique sounds, particularly its 'L' and 'R' variations. Take your time and practice with the daily words!

docs.title: 02. Survival, Logistics, and Emergencies
docs.text: Mastering this core vocabulary will allow you to survive your first few days in Chennai. These aren't just words; they are the communicative tools you need to negotiate, direct transport, fulfill basic needs, and handle critical situations.

docs.title: 1. The Binary Engine of Needs (Want vs. Don't Want)
docs.text:
In a new city, clearly expressing your desires and boundaries is critical.

  • Venum ⟨VEH-noom⟩ (Want / Need): The absolute essential. Use it to request items or express necessity.
    • Usage: "Enakku ⟨eh-nuh-KUU⟩ thanni ⟨THUN-nee⟩ venum" (I want water).
  • Vendaam ⟨VAYN-daam⟩ (Don't want / Don't need): Your shield against unwanted offers, higher fares, or persistent vendors.
    • Usage: "Plastic bag vendaam" (No plastic bag). "Extra muppathu ruba vendaam" (I won't pay 30 rupees extra).

docs.title: 2. Establishing Reality (Presence and Knowledge)
docs.text:
These words help you determine if something exists or if you understand a situation.

  • Illa ⟨ILL-lah⟩ (No / Not here): A powerful word indicating non-existence, absence, or direct disagreement.
    • Usage: "Thanni ⟨THUN-nee⟩ illa" (There is no water).
  • Theriyadhu ⟨they-ree-YAA-dhoo⟩ (I don't know): Highly useful for establishing boundaries regarding local knowledge or language limits.
    • Usage: "Enakku ⟨eh-nuh-KUU⟩ Tamil theriyadhu" (I don't know Tamil).

docs.title: 3. The Commands of Motion (Verbs)
docs.text:
Always remember to add an -nga to the verb root to be polite to strangers and service personnel in Tamil.

  • Vaanga ⟨VAA-ngah⟩ (Come): The respectful imperative of Vaa ⟨VAA⟩. Essential for requesting someone to come over.
  • Ponga ⟨POH-ngah⟩ (Go): The respectful imperative of Po ⟨POH⟩. Crucial for directing transport.
  • Niruthunga ⟨nee-ROOT-thung-gah⟩ (Stop): The standard respectful command for instructing an auto or cab driver to halt.

docs.title: 4. Navigating the Streets (Directions)
docs.text:
• Munnadi ⟨MOON-naa-dee⟩ (Forward / Ahead): Used to indicate forward motion.
  • Pinnadi ⟨PIN-naa-dee⟩ (Behind / Back): Indicates the rear or reversing direction.
  • Valadhu pakkam ⟨vuh-luh-DHOO PUK-kum⟩ (Right side): Made of Valadhu (Right) and pakkam (Side).
  • Idadhu pakkam ⟨ee-duh-DHOO PUK-kum⟩ (Left side): Made of Idadhu (Left) and pakkam (Side).
  • Nera ⟨NAY-rah⟩ (Straight): A directional adverb used extensively in navigation.

docs.title: 5. Transactions and Giving
docs.text:
• Evvalavu aachu? ⟨EV-vuh-luh-voo AAH-choo⟩ (How much did it become?): The critical phrase for all financial transactions and market inquiries.
  • Kudunga ⟨KOO-doong-gah⟩ (Give): The respectful imperative used when requesting items in a shop.
  • Eduthukkonga ⟨eh-doot-tuh-KONG-gah⟩ (Take): Use this respectfully when handing cash or items to a vendor.
  • Konjam kammi pannunga ⟨KON-jum KUM-mee PUN-noong-gah⟩ (Reduce a little): The standard, polite mechanism for initiating bargaining in a market.
  • Vidunga ⟨vee-DOONG-gah⟩ (Leave / Let go): Tell the vendor to keep the change, or use it to drop a subject.

docs.title: 6. Communication Actions
docs.text:
• Sollunga ⟨SOL-loong-gah⟩ (Tell / Say): Used to prompt information. "Tamil la ⟨tuh-MILL luh⟩ sollunga" (Say it in Tamil).
  • Anuppunga ⟨UH-noop-poong-gah⟩ (Send): The respectful imperative. "Location anuppunga" (Send the location).
  • Podunga ⟨POH-doong-gah⟩ (Put): Used physically and mechanically "Meter podunga".
  • Pannunga ⟨PUN-noong-gah⟩ (Do): The universal auxiliary verb. Attach it to any English noun to create a 'Tanglish' verb (e.g., "Google Pay pannunga").

docs.title: 7. Dining and Restaurants
docs.text: Tamil English Meaning Context / Usage Saappittaachaa? ⟨saap-pit-TAACH-chaa⟩ Had lunch/dinner? Universal greeting after "Hello". Menu kudunga ⟨MEN-oo KOO-doong-gah⟩ Give menu Asking for the menu respectfully. Single tea kudunga Give one tea Ordering tea at a local stall. Bill kudunga ⟨BILL KOO-doong-gah⟩ Give bill Finishing the meal. Kaaram kammi pannunga ⟨KAA-rum KUM-mee PUN-noong-gah⟩ Make it less spicy Very useful instruction for restaurants. Saappadu nalla irukku ⟨SAAP-paa-doo NUL-lah ee-ROOK-koo⟩ Food is good "Dosa nalla irukku." (Dosa is good).

docs.title: 8. Household Emergencies
docs.text: Tamil English Meaning Context / Usage Thanni varala ⟨THUN-nee vuh-RUL-lah⟩ Water is not coming Critical plumbing issue. Current illa ⟨current ILL-lah⟩ No power Reporting a power cut. Clean pannunga ⟨clean PUN-noong-gah⟩ Clean it "Room clean pannunga." (Clean the room). Naalaikku vaanga ⟨NAA-lai-koo VAA-ngah⟩ Come tomorrow "Innaikku venam, naalaikku vaanga." (Not today, come tomorrow).

docs.title: 9. Boundary Setting and Conflict
docs.text:
Be extremely careful with these phrases. They are blunt and direct.

Tamil English Meaning Context / Usage Enakku help venum ⟨eh-nuh-KUU help VEH-noom⟩ I need help Emergency situation calling for assistance. Po! / Ponga! ⟨POH / POH-ngah⟩ Go! / Get lost! Po is a rude dismissal. Ponga is the respectful "Please go". Police ah koopiduven ⟨police ah KOO-pid-doo-ven⟩ I will call police A direct threat for severe harassment. En thappu dhaan ⟨EN THUP-poo dhaan⟩ It is my mistake The safest way to apologize to authority or police. 📤 Share

docs.title: 03. Social & Professional
docs.text: Chennai's social grammar is built on respect and indirect politeness. Mastering these phrases will help you transition from a visitor to a local colleague.

docs.title: 1. The Universal Icebreaker
docs.text:
In Chennai, you don't generally ask "How are you?" as a primary greeting. You ask about food.

  • Saappittaachaa? ⟨saap-pit-TAACH-chaa⟩ (Had lunch/dinner?): The #1 Chennai greeting. It acts as a universal icebreaker.
    • Usage: Always asked after saying "Vanakkam" ⟨vuh-nuh-KUM⟩.
  • Nalla irukken ⟨NUL-lah ee-ROOK-ken⟩ (I am fine/good): The standard positive response to inquiries about your well-being (like Eppadi irukkeenga? ⟨epp-puh-DEE ee-ROOK-keeng-gah⟩).

docs.title: 2. Information Gathering (Interrogatives)
docs.text:
• Enna? ⟨EN-nah⟩ (What?): The universal interrogative for objects and concepts; can also express surprise.
    • Usage: "Idhu ⟨ee-DHOO⟩ enna?" (What is this?)
  • Yaaru? ⟨YAA-roo⟩ (Who?): The interrogative used specifically for identifying people.
    • Usage: "Ivaru ⟨ee-VUH-roo⟩ yaaru?" (Who is this person?)
  • Per(u) ⟨PAY-roo⟩ (Name): Essential for introductions.
    • Usage: "Unga ⟨OON-gah⟩ peru enna?" (What is your name? - Respectful)
  • Enga irukkeenga? ⟨ENG-gah ee-ROOK-keeng-gah⟩ (Where are you?):
    • Usage: "Neenga ⟨NEENG-gah⟩ enga irukkeenga?" (Where are you right now?)

docs.title: 3. Office Logistics and Work Culture
docs.text:
Communicating status updates efficiently.

  • Vela ⟨VAY-luh⟩ (Work):
    • Usage: "Enakku ⟨eh-nuh-KUU⟩ vela irukku ⟨ee-ROOK-koo⟩" (I have work).
  • Mudinjithu ⟨moo-DIN-jid-dhoo⟩ (Finished/Over):
    • Usage: "Meeting mudinjithu" (Meeting over). "Vela mudinjithu" (Work is finished).
  • Konjam ⟨KON-jum⟩ (A little): A highly utilized adverb that functions as a polite conversational softener.
    • Usage: "Konjam busy ah irukken ⟨ee-ROOK-ken⟩" (I am a little busy).
  • Nadanga ⟨NUH-dung-gah⟩ (Walk/Move/Go):
    • Usage: "Meeting-ku nadanga" (Let's walk to the meeting).

docs.title: 4. Time Management and Scheduling
docs.text:
• Innaikku ⟨IN-nai-koo⟩ (Today):
    • Usage: "Innaikku leave ⟨LEAVE⟩ irukku" (Today is a holiday).
  • Naalaikku ⟨NAA-lai-koo⟩ (Tomorrow): Often used to politely postpone meetings or visits.
    • Usage: "Naalaikku paakkalaam ⟨PAAK-kuh-laam⟩" (Let's meet tomorrow).

docs.title: 5. Joining Ideas (Conjunctions)
docs.text:
These words are the glue for building more complex, natural-sounding sentences.

  • Aprom ⟨up-PROM⟩ (And / Then / Later): Used to link similar items in lists or connect parallel ideas.
    • Usage: "Tea aprom biscuit" (Tea and biscuit).
  • Aanaa ⟨AAH-naa⟩ (But): Essential for introducing contrast.
    • Usage: "Venum ⟨VEH-noom⟩, aanaa illa ⟨ILL-lah⟩" (Want, but don't have).
  • Illaati ⟨ILL-laa-tee⟩ (Or / Otherwise): Used for presenting options or choices.
    • Usage: "Coffee illaati tea?" (Coffee or tea?)
  • Apdidhaan ⟨up-dee-DHAAN⟩ (Like that):
    • Usage: "Apdidhaan pannunga ⟨PUN-noong-gah⟩" (Do it like that).

docs.title: 6. The Cultural Goodbye
docs.text:
In Tamil culture, you rarely say a definitive "I am going" (Naan poren ⟨NAAN POH-ren⟩) when leaving a place, as it is historically considered inauspicious (implying you are leaving and never returning).

Instead, the culturally accurate and polite way to take leave is to say:

"Naan poyittu varen" ⟨NAAN POY-itt-too VAH-ren⟩ (I will go and come).

  • Literal Meaning: "Having gone, I will come back."
  • Context / Usage: Use this when leaving a friend's house, wrapping up a conversational exchange, or saying goodbye to colleagues. It signifies respect, optimism, and the implicit promise that the relationship or connection will continue.
📤 Share

docs.title: 04. Time, Numbers & Calendar
docs.text: Mastering the numbers 1 through 10 unlocks the logic for the entire numerical system.

docs.title: 1. Core Numerical Logic
docs.text:
Learning the base numbers allows you to manage fares, quantities, and addresses.

  • 0: Poojiyam ⟨POO-jee-yum⟩
  • 1: Onnu ⟨ON-noo⟩
  • 2: Rendu ⟨REN-doo⟩
  • 3: Moonu ⟨MOO-noo⟩
  • 4: Naalu ⟨NAA-loo⟩
  • 5: Anju ⟨UN-joo⟩
  • 6: Aaru ⟨AA-roo⟩
  • 7: Yezhu ⟨YAY-zhoo⟩
  • 8: Ettu ⟨ET-too⟩
  • 9: Ombodhu ⟨OM-buh-dhoo⟩
  • 10: Paththu ⟨PUT-too⟩

docs.title: The 'Teens' and Multiples of Ten
docs.text:
• 11: Pathin-onnu ⟨pu-dhin-ON-noo⟩ (Paththu + Onnu).
  • 12: Pannerendu ⟨pun-NIH-ren-doo⟩
  • 20: Iruvathu ⟨ee-ROO-vuh-dhoo⟩
  • 30: Muppathu ⟨MOOP-puh-dhoo⟩
  • 40: Naappathu ⟨NAAP-puh-dhoo⟩
  • 50: Ambathu ⟨UM-buh-dhoo⟩
  • 60: Aruvathu ⟨UH-roo-vuh-dhoo⟩
  • 100: Nooru ⟨NOO-roo⟩. Vital for larger transactions and rent discussions.
  • 1000: Aayiram ⟨AA-yee-rum⟩.
  • Lakh: Latcham ⟨LUT-chum⟩.

docs.title: 2. Fractions and Measurements
docs.text:
Essential for buying vegetables or telling precise time.

  • Ara ⟨UH-rah⟩ (Half):
    • Usage: "Ara mani neram ⟨MUH-nee NAY-rum⟩" (Half an hour).
  • Kaal ⟨KAAL⟩ (Quarter):
    • Usage: "Moonu kaal ⟨MOO-noo KAAL⟩" (3:15 / Three and a quarter).
  • Muk-kaal ⟨MOOK-KAAL⟩ (3/4th):
    • Usage: "Moonu muk-kaal ⟨MOO-noo MOOK-KAAL⟩" (3:45).

docs.title: 3. Time and Scheduling Logistics
docs.text:
These nouns and adverbs allow you to coordinate meetings and set expectations.

  • Neram ⟨NAY-rum⟩ (Time): Noun used to ask duration or exact clock time.
    • Usage: "Neram enna ⟨EN-nah⟩?" (What is the time?)
  • Mani ⟨MUH-nee⟩ (Hour / Bell):
    • Usage: "Oru ⟨OH-roo⟩ mani" (1 o'clock).
  • Nimisham ⟨NEE-mee-shum⟩ (Minute):
    • Usage: "Paththu ⟨PUT-too⟩ nimisham" (10 minutes).
  • Ippo ⟨IP-poh⟩ (Now): Temporal adverb indicating the present moment.
    • Usage: "Ippo vaanga ⟨VAA-ngah⟩" (Come right now).
  • Aprom ⟨up-PROM⟩ (Later): Extremely common in delaying tactics or scheduling.
    • Usage: "Aprom vaanga" (Come later).

docs.title: Days of the Week
docs.text:
• Sunday: Gnayiru ⟨GNAA-yee-roo⟩
  • Monday: Thingal ⟨THIN-gul⟩
  • Tuesday: Sevvai ⟨SEV-vaay⟩
  • Wednesday: Budhan ⟨BOO-dhun⟩
  • Thursday: Viyaazhan ⟨vee-YAA-zhun⟩
  • Friday: Velli ⟨VEL-lee⟩
  • Saturday: Sani ⟨SUH-nee⟩

docs.title: The Daily Calendar
docs.text:
• Kaalai ⟨KAA-lai⟩ (Morning): "Kaalaila 8 mani" (Morning 8 AM).
  • Madhiyam ⟨MUD-hee-yum⟩ (Afternoon): "Madhiyam saappadu ⟨SAAP-paa-doo⟩" (Afternoon lunch).
  • Saayangaalam ⟨SAA-yun-gaa-lum⟩ (Evening): "Saayangaalam tea" (Evening tea).
  • Rathiri ⟨RAAH-thee-ree⟩ (Night): "Rathiri saappadu" (Dinner).
  • Innaikku ⟨IN-nai-koo⟩ (Today): "Innaikku leave ⟨LEAVE⟩ irukku" (Today is a holiday).
  • Naalaikku ⟨NAA-lai-koo⟩ (Tomorrow): "Naalaikku paakkalaam ⟨PAAK-kuh-laam⟩" (Let's meet tomorrow).
  • Neththu ⟨NAY-thoo⟩ (Yesterday): "Neththu vandhaanga ⟨VUN-dhaan-gah⟩" (Came yesterday).
  • Naalaannaikku ⟨NAA-laan-nai-koo⟩ (Day after tomorrow): "Naalaannaikku vaanga" (Come the day after).
📤 Share

docs.title: 05. Core Verbs & Grammar
docs.text: Memorizing the verb root alongside its imperative (command) form allows for immediate action. Crucial Rule: Appending -nga to the verb root ensures the request is polite. Use this default for everyone except close friends or children.

docs.title: 1. Core Action Verbs (The Engines of Communication)
docs.text:
These verbs handle physical movement, giving, and taking.

  • Vaa / Vaanga ⟨VAA / VAA-ngah⟩ (Come): Vaanga is the required formal application.
    • Usage: "Ulla ⟨ULL-lah⟩ vaanga" (Please come inside).
  • Po / Ponga ⟨POH / POH-ngah⟩ (Go): Essential for directing transport.
  • Kudu / Kudunga ⟨KOO-doo / KOO-doong-gah⟩ (Give): Used when requesting items in a shop or asking for a ticket.
  • Edu / Edunga ⟨EH-doo / EH-doong-gah⟩ (Take): Used when handing cash or items to a vendor.
  • Pannu / Pannunga ⟨PUN-noo / PUN-noong-gah⟩ (Do): The universal Tanglish auxiliary verb.
  • Paaru / Paarunga ⟨PAA-roo / PAA-roong-gah⟩ (See / Look): Use it to direct someone's attention.
  • Podu / Podunga ⟨POH-doo / POH-doong-gah⟩ (Put / Drop): Used to pour liquids or request mechanical action (Meter podunga).
  • Thora / Thoranga ⟨THOH-rah / THOH-rang-gah⟩ (Open):
    • Usage: "Kadhava ⟨KUH-dhuh-vuh⟩ thoranga" (Open the door).
  • Vidu / Vidunga ⟨VEE-doo / VEE-doong-gah⟩ (Leave / Let go):
    • Usage: "Change vidunga" (Keep the change).
  • Niruthu / Niruthunga ⟨nee-ROOT-thoo / nee-ROOT-thoong-gah⟩ (Stop): The standard respectful command for halting transport.
    • Usage: "Inga ⟨ING-gah⟩ niruthunga" (Stop here).
  • Iru / Irunga ⟨ee-ROO / ee-ROONG-gah⟩ (Be / Wait): The existential verb.
    • Usage: "Summa ⟨SOOM-mah⟩ irunga" (Just be / Keep quiet).

docs.title: 2. Communication and Data Processing
docs.text:
These verbs handle speech, information transfer, and instruction.

  • Sollu / Sollunga ⟨SOL-loo / SOL-loong-gah⟩ (Tell / Say): Used to prompt information.
  • Kelu / Kelunga ⟨KAY-loo / KAY-loong-gah⟩ (Ask / Listen): Used to gain attention.
  • Anuppu / Anuppunga ⟨UH-noop-poo / UH-noop-poong-gah⟩ (Send): "Message anuppunga"
  • Koopidu / Koopidunga ⟨KOO-pee-doo / KOO-pee-doong-gah⟩ (Call): "Avangala ⟨uh-vung-guh-LUH⟩ koopidunga"
  • Ezhudhu / Ezhudhunga ⟨EH-zhoo-dhoo / EH-zhoo-dhoong-gah⟩ (Write): "Peru ⟨PAY-roo⟩ ezhudhunga"
  • Padi / Padinga ⟨PUH-dee / PUH-deeng-gah⟩ (Read): "Idha ⟨ee-DHAH⟩ padinga"

docs.title: 3. Food and Consumption
docs.text:
• Saappidu / Saappidunga ⟨SAAP-pee-doo / SAAP-pee-doong-gah⟩ (Eat):
    • Usage: "Saappadu ⟨SAAP-paa-doo⟩ saappidunga" (Eat food).
  • Kudi / Kudinga ⟨KOO-dee / KOO-deeng-gah⟩ (Drink):
    • Usage: "Thanni ⟨THUN-nee⟩ kudinga" (Drink water).

docs.title: 4. Basic Sentence Forms (Present & Past)
docs.text:
Finite verbs in Tamil must precisely mirror the subject.

English Sentence Tamil Translation Context / Tense Focus I am going now. Naan ⟨NAAN⟩ ippo ⟨IP-poh⟩ poren ⟨POH-ren⟩. Present (1st Person): Root (Po) + Pronoun (-en). "I am going." They came yesterday. Avanga ⟨UH-vung-gah⟩ neththu ⟨NAY-thoo⟩ vandhaanga ⟨VUN-dhaan-gah⟩. Past (3rd Person Plural): Root (Vaa) past tense form. "They came." I will do it tomorrow. Naalaikku ⟨NAA-lai-koo⟩ pannuven ⟨PUN-noo-ven⟩. Future Intent: Root (Pannu) + Future + Pronoun. "I will do." Look, then speak. Paarunga ⟨PAA-roong-gah⟩, aprom ⟨up-PROM⟩ sollunga ⟨SOL-loong-gah⟩. Sequential Commands: Use the respectful imperative chained together. 📤 Share

docs.title: 5. Core Conjunctions (Sentence Linking)
docs.text:
These words form the backbone of complex conversational Tamil.

Tamil English Meaning Context / Usage Aprom ⟨up-PROM⟩ And / Then / Later Used to link items (Idli aprom Vada) or ask "Then what?" (Aprom enna?). Aanaa ⟨AAH-naa⟩ But Essential for contrast. "Naan vandhen ⟨VUN-dhen⟩, aanaa neenga illa ⟨ILL-lah⟩." (I came, but you weren't there). Illaati ⟨ILL-laa-tee⟩ Or Used for options. "Coffee illaati Tea?" (Coffee or Tea?). Yenna / Yen-naa ⟨YEN-naa⟩ Because "Naan varala ⟨VUH-ruh-lah⟩, yenna vela ⟨VAY-lah⟩ irundhuchu ⟨ee-ROON-dhoo-choo⟩." (I didn't come because there was work). Adhanaala ⟨uh-dhuh-NAA-lah⟩ Therefore / So "Mazhai ⟨MUH-zhai⟩ vandhuchu ⟨VUN-dhoo-choo⟩, adhanaala traffic." (Rain came, so traffic).

docs.title: 6. Advanced Pronoun & Tense Quick Reference
docs.text:
While you know the basic pronouns, here is how they pair with the future tense auxiliary verbs for the root Pannu (to do), Po (to go), and Vaa (to come).

  • Naan ⟨NAAN⟩ (I) -> Pannuven ⟨PUN-noo-ven⟩ (I will do), Poven ⟨POH-ven⟩ (I will go), Varuven ⟨vuh-ROO-ven⟩ (I will come).
  • Naama ⟨NAA-mah⟩ (We) -> Pannuvom ⟨PUN-noo-vom⟩ (We will do).
  • Nee ⟨NEE⟩ (You - Casual) -> Pannuva? ⟨PUN-noo-vah⟩ (Will you do?).
  • Neenga ⟨NEENG-gah⟩ (You - Respectful) -> Pannuveenga? ⟨PUN-noo-veeng-gah⟩ (Will you do?).
  • Avan ⟨UH-vun⟩ (He) -> Pannuvaan ⟨PUN-noo-vaan⟩ (He will do).
  • Ava ⟨UH-vah⟩ (She) -> Pannuvaal ⟨PUN-noo-vaal⟩ (She will do).
  • Avanga ⟨UH-vung-gah⟩ (They / Respectful He/She) -> Pannuvaanga ⟨PUN-noo-vaang-gah⟩ (They will do).
  • Adhu ⟨UH-dhoo⟩ (It - Neuter) -> Pannum ⟨PUN-noom⟩ (It will do).

docs.title: 06. Adjectives & Descriptors
docs.text:
In Tamil, descriptive adjectives generally precede the noun they modify, maintaining the exact same syntactic order as English and Hindi (e.g., Periya veedu ⟨pay-REE-yuh VEE-doo⟩ = Big house = Bada ghar).

This lesson covers high-frequency opposites and essential descriptors for size, quality, and taste.

docs.title: 1. Opposites and General Descriptors
docs.text:
These adjectives are crucial for evaluating objects, making purchases, or describing the environment.

Tamil English Meaning Context / Notes Nalla ⟨NUL-lah⟩ Good / Nice A universally useful adjective. Before a noun (e.g., Nalla paiyan). Nalla irukku ⟨NUL-lah ee-ROOK-koo⟩ It is good Used to describe food, weather, health, or objects. Mosa(m) ⟨MOH-sum⟩ Bad Used to describe poor quality or negative situations. Periya ⟨pay-REE-yuh⟩ Big Always precedes the noun (e.g., Periya car). Chinna ⟨CHIN-nah⟩ Small Essential for determining portion size or items. Sooda ⟨SOO-dah⟩ Hot Used for objects, and food (e.g., Soodana kaapi - Hot coffee). Kulira / Jil-nu ⟨koo-LEE-ruh / JILL-noo⟩ Cold Used for liquids or weather ("Jil-nu irukku"). Pudusu ⟨POO-doo-soo⟩ New Used for new objects or places (e.g., Pudusu car). Pazhasu ⟨PUH-zhuh-soo⟩ Old Used for old things. Note the proto-Dravidian 'zh' retroflex. Vegama ⟨VAY-guh-mah⟩ Fast Very useful to urge auto/cab drivers to speed up (Vegama ponga). Medhuva ⟨MAY-dhoo-vah⟩ Slow / Softly Extremely important when asking someone to drive carefully or speak slower. Jaasthi / Romba ⟨JAAS-tee / ROM-bah⟩ More / Too much Essential for bargaining (Jaasthi aachu - It has become too much). Kammi ⟨KUM-mee⟩ Less Used to negotiate prices (Konjam kammi pannunga - Make it a little less). Sari ⟨SUH-ree⟩ Correct / Okay Used constantly as an agreement particle to confirm instructions or a price. Thappu ⟨THUP-poo⟩ Wrong Used to point out an error. Suththam ⟨SOOT-thum⟩ Clean Sanskrit root (Swachh) Azhukku ⟨UH-zhook-koo⟩ Dirty Used to complain about hygiene.

docs.title: 2. Sensory Experiences (Taste)
docs.text:
The five primary tastes. These are simple adjectives that can be used directly with nouns or with the verb irukku ⟨ee-ROOK-koo⟩ (it is).

Tamil English Meaning Example / Context Inippu ⟨ee-NIP-poo⟩ Sweet "Coffee romba ⟨ROM-bah⟩ inippa irukku." (Coffee is very sweet). Kaaram ⟨KAA-rum⟩ Spicy / Hot "Oota ⟨OO-tah⟩ konjam ⟨KON-jum⟩ kaaram venum ⟨VEH-noom⟩." (I want the food a little spicy). Pulippu ⟨poo-LIP-poo⟩ Sour "Thayir ⟨THUH-yeer⟩ pulippa irukku." (The curd has become sour). Kasappu ⟨kuh-SUP-poo⟩ Bitter Used for bitter gourd or medicines. Uppu ⟨OOP-poo⟩ Salt / Salty (Also the noun for salt). Rusi ⟨ROO-see⟩ Tasty / Delicious "Saappadu romba rusiya irundhuchu ⟨ee-ROON-dhoo-choo⟩." (Food was very tasty).

Note: Complex internal states and physical ailments (like hunger, pain, anger) are typically expressed using the "Experiencer Dative Paradigm" (e.g., 'Enakku ⟨eh-nuh-KOO⟩ vali ⟨VUH-lee⟩ irukku' - To me, there is pain).

📤 Share

docs.title: 07. Chennai Slang & Tanglish
docs.text:
Chennai spoken Tamil is a highly dynamic dialect. It seamlessly blends formal Tamil with English (forming "Tanglish"), and features a rich tapestry of local street slang known as Madras Bashai.

Mastering this localized vocabulary is essential for social integration and daily logistics.

docs.title: 1. The Hierarchy of Respect and Brotherhood
docs.text:
Chennai values linguistic politeness, but also has distinct terms for close peers.

Tanglish / Slang Literal Meaning Chennai Context and Usage Macha / Machi ⟨MUH-chah / MUH-chee⟩ Brother-in-law Universal term for a close male friend or peer. ("Bro/Dude"). Extremely common across Tamil Nadu. Thala ⟨THUH-lah⟩ Head / Leader A highly respectful, yet casual term used for bosses, actors, or even auto drivers who perform well. Boss / Ji ⟨BOSS / JEE⟩ Leader / Sir Universal term of address for auto drivers, waiters, or strangers. Thambi ⟨THUM-bee⟩ Younger brother Used fondly to address a younger male waiter, driver, or shop assistant. Saappittaachaa? ⟨saap-pit-TAACH-chaa⟩ Had food? The primary social icebreaker used immediately after hello. "Saappittaachaa boss?" Enna thala, epdi irukkeenga? ⟨EN-nah THUH-lah, ep-DEE ee-ROOK-keeng-gah⟩ What boss, how are you? Highly casual greeting among peers to establish immediate friendly rapport.

docs.title: 2. Tanglish and The "Pannu" Engine
docs.text:
Tanglish effortlessly integrates English nouns and verbs into Tamil. This is most commonly achieved by appending a euphonic -u to English nouns (e.g., "Car-u", "Office-u") or attaching the universal action verb pannunga ⟨PUN-noong-gah⟩ (do) to English verbs.

Tanglish Term Meaning Chennai Context and Usage Adjust pannunga ⟨adjust PUN-noong-gah⟩ Please accommodate A cultural cornerstone. Used to ask for physical space on a bus, a favor, or a compromise. "Konjam adjust pannunga." Drop pannunga ⟨drop PUN-noong-gah⟩ Give a lift "Metro kitta ⟨KIT-tah⟩ drop pannunga." (Drop me near the metro). Dead end-la left-u ⟨dead end-LA left-U⟩ Take a left at the dead end Using spatial English nouns with Tamil suffixes to give directions. Scene illa ⟨scene ILL-lah⟩ No chance / No issue Used to dismiss a problem or highlight someone showing off. "Romba scene podadha ⟨POH-daa-dhah⟩." (Don't act like a big shot).

docs.title: 3. Street Descriptors (The Good and the Bad)
docs.text: Slang Term English Meaning Chennai Context and Usage Semma / Vera level ⟨SEM-mah / VAY-ruh LEVEL⟩ Awesome / Next Level Used to describe anything of excellent quality (food, movies). "Movie semmaya irundhuchu ⟨ee-ROON-dhoo-choo⟩" Mass ⟨MAAS⟩ Amazing / Grand Describes a performance, hero entry, or object that is spectacular. Mokka ⟨MOK-kah⟩ Boring / Useless Completely tedious, broken, or a terrible joke. "Mokka padam ⟨PUH-dum⟩." (Boring movie). Daridram ⟨duh-RID-drum⟩ Bad luck / Annoying A nuisance, an argument, or a terrible situation. "Enna daridram ba ⟨bah⟩." Gethu ⟨GET-thoo⟩ Swag / Attitude Context matters. Refers to someone's confident, cool style. "Avan geththu kaatraan ⟨KAAT-raan⟩." (He is showing off his swag). Full-bodhai ⟨full-BOH-dhai⟩ Drunk / Intoxicated "Avan full bodhaila irukkan ⟨ee-ROOK-kaan⟩." (He is completely drunk). Vittudu ⟨VIT-too-doo⟩ Let it go / Leave it Used to drop an argument or dismiss a topic. "Adha vittudu ba ⟨bah⟩." (Leave that topic). 📤 Share

docs.title: 08. Travel, Directions, and Conflict
docs.text: Navigating Chennai traffic requires clear, authoritative directions. The core spatial markers you learned previously (inga ⟨IN-gah⟩, anga ⟨UN-gah⟩) are combined with these directional commands to form the "Auto-Rickshaw Protocol". Handling interactions on the street, including negotiations and minor conflicts, is also crucial.

docs.title: 1. The Auto-Rickshaw Protocol
docs.text:
Before boarding, always establish the destination and the pricing model.

  • Destination Check: Always ask before boarding: "[Location] varuveengala?" ⟨VAH-roo-VEEN-guh-lah⟩ (Will you come to [Location]?).
  • Pricing Demand: Establish the rate: "Meter podu" ⟨MEE-ter POH-doo⟩ (Put the meter down) or "Evlo aagum?" ⟨YEV-loh AAH-gum⟩ (How much will it be?).
  • Refusing Surge Pricing: "Adhellam venam" ⟨a-dhay-LAAM VAY-naam⟩ (I don't want all that / No need).

docs.title: 2. Navigating the Streets (Directions)
docs.text:
Use these terms with the respectful imperative verbs like Ponga ⟨POHN-gah⟩ (Go) or Yedunga ⟨YAY-doon-gah⟩ (Take) or Niruthunga ⟨NEE-roo-THOON-gah⟩ (Stop).

Tamil English Meaning Context / Usage Nera ⟨NAY-rah⟩ Straight "Nera ponga" (Go straight). Left-la ⟨LEFT-lah⟩ Left Side Chennai heavily mixes English directions. "Left-la yedunga" (Take a left). Right-la ⟨RIGHT-lah⟩ Right Side "Right-la yedunga" (Take a right). Munnadi ⟨MOON-naa-dee⟩ Forward / Ahead "Konjam ⟨KOHN-jum⟩ munnadi niruthunga" (Stop a little ahead). Pinnadi ⟨PIN-naa-dee⟩ Behind / Back Indicates the rear or reversing direction. "Pinnadi vaanga ⟨VAAN-gah⟩" (Come back/reverse). Inga ⟨IN-gah⟩ Here "Ingaye niruthunga" (Stop right here). Anga ⟨UN-gah⟩ There "Anga niruthunga" (Stop there). Vegama ponga ⟨VAY-gah-maa POHN-gah⟩ Go fast "Late aagudhu ⟨AAH-goo-dhoo⟩, vegama ponga" (It's getting late, go fast). Medhuva ponga ⟨MAY-dhoo-vaa POHN-gah⟩ Go slow Useful for rough roads or navigating narrow gullies.

docs.title: 3. Distance and Space
docs.text: Tamil English Meaning Context / Usage Pakkathula ⟨PUK-kah-thoo-lah⟩ Near "Pakkathula dhaan irukku" (It is right near). Dhoorama ⟨DHOO-rah-maa⟩ Far "Romba ⟨ROHM-bah⟩ dhooram" (Very far). Ulla ⟨OOL-lah⟩ Inside Useful for directing delivery personnel to leave items inside a gate. Veliya ⟨VAY-lee-yah⟩ Outside Used to instruct drivers or guests to wait externally. Vazhi illa ⟨VAH-zhee ILL-lah⟩ Dead End Literally: Road is not there / No road ahead.

docs.title: 4. Traffic and Road Rage
docs.text: Tamil English Meaning Context / Usage Paathu ponga ⟨PAA-thoo POHN-gah⟩ Go carefully Used when someone is driving carelessly. Vazhi vidunga ⟨VAH-zhee VIH-doon-gah⟩ Leave way Asking for space to pass. Signal-la niruthunga ⟨signal-lah NEE-roo-THOON-gah⟩ Stop at the signal Auto/Cab instruction. Evlo fine? ⟨YEV-loh fine⟩ How much fine? Interaction with traffic police. Licence irukku ⟨licence IH-roo-koo⟩ I have a licence Showing documentation. Parking enga irukku? ⟨parking YEN-gah IH-roo-koo⟩ Where is parking? Finding a spot.

docs.title: 5. Conflict De-escalation
docs.text:
The goal here is to soothe tension rather than amplify it.

Tamil English Meaning Context / Usage Summa irunga ⟨SOOM-maa IH-roon-gah⟩ Be quiet / Just be "Saar, summa irunga." (Sir, just be quiet). Adhigama pesaathinga ⟨uh-DHEE-guh-maa PAY-saa-theen-gah⟩ Don't talk too much Used to stop an argument from stretching. Paravala ⟨PAH-rah-vaa-la⟩ No problem / It's okay Ideal for resolving minor bumps or mistakes. En thappu illa ⟨YEN THUP-poo ILL-lah⟩ Not my mistake Firmly defending yourself. Emathureenga ⟨YAY-maa-thoo-reen-gah⟩ Cheating "Idhu yemaathuradhu." (This is cheating).

docs.title: 6. Medical Emergencies
docs.text:
If you need to quickly communicate distress to an auto driver, pharmacist, or bystander, these phrases are universally understood.

Tamil English Meaning Context / Usage Vegama hospital-ku ponga ⟨VAY-gah-maa hospital-KOO POHN-gah⟩ Go to the hospital fast Instructing an auto or cab driver. Thalai vali ⟨THAH-lai VAH-lee⟩ Head ache General distress (Thalai = Head, Vali = Pain). Kaachal ⟨KAAH-chul⟩ Fever Asking for basic medicine at a pharmacy. Ratham varudhu ⟨RAH-thum VAH-roo-dhoo⟩ Bleeding is happening Accidental emergency (Ratham = Blood). Doctor enga irukaanga? ⟨doctor YEN-gah ih-ROO-kaan-gah⟩ Where is the doctor? Finding help in a clinic.

docs.title: 7. Home and Vehicle Repairs
docs.text: Tamil English Meaning Context / Usage Puncture aayiduchu ⟨puncture AAH-yee-doo-choo⟩ It got punctured Tyre trouble. Thanni olugudhu ⟨THUN-nee OH-loo-goo-dhoo⟩ Water is leaking Plumbing issue. Pipe block aayiduchu ⟨pipe block AAH-yee-doo-choo⟩ Pipe got blocked Drain issue. Light eriyala ⟨light YEH-ree-yah-lah⟩ Light is not burning Electrical issue. Fan odala ⟨fan OH-duh-lah⟩ Fan not running Broken appliance. Switch work aagala ⟨switch work AAH-guh-lah⟩ Switch not working Broken switch. Evlo aagum? ⟨YEV-loh AAH-gum⟩ How much will it be? Asking for an estimate before work begins. 📤 Share

docs.title: 09. Everyday Logistics & Services
docs.text:
While English is sufficient for navigating corporate spaces in OMR or modern hospitals in Chennai, the city's vast network of delivery apps, service professionals, and housing logistics operates heavily in Tamil.

This module covers the practical, street-level vocabulary needed to manage everyday logistics, interact with landlords, call for repairs, and navigate large IT parks.

docs.title: The "Adjust Pannikonga" Philosophy
docs.text: Before diving into vocabulary, understand the cultural cornerstone of Chennai: "Konjam adjust pannikonga" ⟨KOHN-jum adjust PUN-nee-kohn-gah⟩ (Please adjust/compromise a little). Whether you are asking an auto driver for 5 rupees change, negotiating with a plumber, or asking someone to budge on a crowded bus, this phrase diffuses tension and signals a cooperative attitude.

docs.title: 1. Delivery Logistics (Swiggy, Zomato, Amazon)
docs.text:
Delivery drivers frequently rely on landmarks rather than precise GPS pins.

English Tamil Notes Come inside Ulla vaanga ⟨OOL-lah VAAN-gah⟩ Use for securely gated areas. Stay outside Veliya nillunga ⟨VAY-lee-yah NIL-loon-gah⟩ To instruct them to wait at the gate. I have sent the location Location anupiten ⟨location ah-NOOP-pee-ten⟩ A vital phrase when they call to ask your exact address. I have paid online Online-la pay panniten ⟨online-lah pay PUN-nee-ten⟩ Extremely common Tanglish. Leave it at the gate Gate kitta vachidungo ⟨gate kit-TAH vuh-CHIH-doon-goh⟩ Instructing to drop the package. I am coming right now Naan ippove varen ⟨Naan ip-POH-vay VAH-ren⟩ Adding "-ve" emphasizes "right now/immediately."

docs.title: 2. Housing & Renting
docs.text:
Managing a household requires coordination with landlords, security guards, and maids.

English Tamil Notes House / Room Veedu / Room ⟨VEE-doo / room⟩ Rent Vaadagai ⟨VAA-duh-guy⟩ Critical for lease discussions (Vaadagai evlo? - How much is the rent?). Water Thanni ⟨THUN-nee⟩ Confirm utility availability (Thanni irukka? ⟨THUN-nee ih-ROO-kah⟩ - Is there water?). Electricity / Power Current Very common Tanglish usage (Current poyiduchu ⟨current POH-yee-doo-choo⟩ - Power is out). Trash Kuppai ⟨KOOP-pye⟩ Directing waste disposal (Kuppai podunga ⟨KOOP-pye POH-doon-gah⟩ - Throw the trash). Sweep the floor Kootunga ⟨KOO-toon-gah⟩ Asking the maid to sweep. Mop/Wipe the floor Thodaiyunga ⟨THOH-dai-yoon-gah⟩ Asking the maid to mop.

docs.title: 3. Repairs & Maintenance
docs.text:
Explaining an issue to a tradesman (plumber, electrician) is often difficult without basic Tamil terminology.

English Tamil Notes Broken / Not working Odanji pochu / Work aagala ⟨OH-dun-jee POH-choo / work AAH-guh-lah⟩ "Work aagala" is the universal Tanglish phrase for malfunctioning items. Pipe is leaking Pipe leak aagudhu ⟨pipe leak AAH-goo-dhoo⟩ Using the English noun with the Tamil continuous verb suffix. Come tomorrow morning Naalaikku kaalaila vaanga ⟨NAA-laik-koo kaa-LAI-lah VAAN-gah⟩ Setting a service appointment. Do it properly Olunga pannunga ⟨oh-LOON-gah PUN-noon-gah⟩ A polite framework for high-quality work. How much will you charge? Evlo aagum? ⟨YEV-loh AAH-gum⟩ Literally "How much will it become?". Reduce it a little Konjam kammi pannunga ⟨KOHN-jum KUM-mee PUN-noon-gah⟩ Standard negotiation phrase in T. Nagar or Parry's Corner.

docs.title: 4. Office Campus & Commuting
docs.text:
Navigating a massive tech park involves short, functional interactions with security and transport staff.

English Tamil Notes Go straight, then left Nera ponga, apram left ⟨NAY-rah POHN-gah, UHP-rum left⟩ Standard directional instructions. Stop here Inga niruthunga ⟨IN-gah NEE-roo-THOON-gah⟩ I work here Naan inga vela pakuren ⟨Naan IN-gah VAY-lah PAA-koo-ren⟩ Explaining your presence to a new security guard. ID Card ID Card Universally understood in Tanglish (e.g., ID Card irukku ⟨ih-ROO-koo⟩ - I have the ID card). Do you have time? (Are you free?) Time irukka? ⟨time ih-ROO-kah⟩ A polite way to interrupt a colleague or ask a shuttle driver if they are ready to depart. 📤 Share

docs.title: 10. Household, Groceries, and Logistics
docs.text: A core lexicon is required to manage the home, instruct domestic help, rent accommodation, and navigate grocery shopping effectively.

docs.title: 1. Renting and Administration
docs.text: Tamil English Meaning Spoken Hindi Parallel Context / Usage Vaadagai ⟨VAA-duh-guy⟩ Rent Kiraya "Vaadagai evlo?" (How much rent?) Advance ⟨ud-VAANCE⟩ Advance / Deposit Deposit "Advance pathu maasam." (10 months advance). Agreement Agreement Karar "Agreement podunga." (Make the agreement). Gaali ⟨GAA-lee⟩ Empty / Vacate Khali "Veedu ⟨VEE-doo⟩ gaaliya irukka?" (Is the house available/empty?) Current Electricity Bijli "Current poyiduchu ⟨POH-yee-doo-choo⟩." (Power is gone). Kanakku ⟨KUH-nuh-koo⟩ Account Khaata "Kanakku ezudhunga." (Write the account/tab). Kaiyezhuthu ⟨KAI-yez-hoo-thoo⟩ Signature Hastakshar "Inga ⟨IN-gah⟩ kaiyezhuthu podunga." (Sign here). Kaasu / Panam ⟨KAA-soo / PUH-num⟩ Money Paisa "Kaasu kudunga ⟨koo-DOON-gah⟩." (Give money).

docs.title: 2. Household Chores and Domestic Management
docs.text:
Directing domestic help requires specific vocabulary, particularly distinguishing between different types of washing and cleaning.

Tamil English Meaning Context / Usage Kuppai kootunga ⟨KOOP-pye KOO-toon-gah⟩ Sweep the floor Kuppai (trash) + kootunga (sweep). Thodaiyunga ⟨THOH-dai-yoon-gah⟩ Wipe the floor Floor mopping command. Paathram kazhuvunga ⟨PAA-thrum kuh-ZHOO-voon-gah⟩ Wash vessels Paathram (vessels) + kazhuvunga (wash objects). Thuni thovaiyunga ⟨THOO-nee thoh-VAI-yoon-gah⟩ Wash clothes Thuni (clothes) + thovaiyunga (wash fabrics). Kuppai podunga ⟨KOOP-pye POH-doon-gah⟩ Throw trash Kuppai (trash) + podunga (put/throw). Kadhavu thiranga ⟨KAH-dhuh-voo thih-RUN-gah⟩ Open door Kadhavu (door) + thiranga (open).

docs.title: 3. Personal Services (Salon & Tailor)
docs.text: Tamil English Meaning Context / Usage Mudi ⟨MOO-dee⟩ Hair "Mudi romba ⟨ROHM-bah⟩ irukku." (Hair is too long/much). Thaadi ⟨THAA-dee⟩ Beard "Thaadi edunga ⟨EH-doon-gah⟩." (Remove beard / Shave). Kuttaiya ⟨KOOT-tai-yah⟩ Short / Small "Kuttaiya vettunga." (Cut it short). Alavu ⟨uh-LUH-voo⟩ Measurement "Alavu edunga ⟨EH-doon-gah⟩." (Take measurement).

docs.title: 4. The Kitchen and Groceries
docs.text: Essential vocabulary for the local market or instructing a cook.

docs.title: Staples
docs.text:
• Arisi ⟨ah-RIH-see⟩ (Rice - Raw)
  • Saadham ⟨SAA-dhum⟩ (Rice - Cooked)
  • Paal ⟨PAAL⟩ (Milk)
  • Thayir ⟨THUH-yeer⟩ (Curd/Yogurt)
  • Uppu ⟨oop-POO⟩ (Salt)
  • Sakkara ⟨SUK-kuh-rah⟩ (Sugar)
  • Ennai ⟨YEN-nai⟩ (Oil)
  • Thanni ⟨THUN-nee⟩ (Water)

docs.title: Vegetables and Fruits (Kaaikari ⟨kaah-KAA-ree⟩ & Pazham ⟨PUH-zhum⟩)
docs.text: Tamil English Meaning Vengayam ⟨ven-GAA-yum⟩ Onion Poondu ⟨POON-doo⟩ Garlic Inji ⟨IN-jee⟩ Ginger Kothamalli ⟨koh-THUM-mul-lee⟩ Coriander Karuveppilai ⟨KUH-roo-VAY-pil-lai⟩ Curry leaves Milagai ⟨Mih-luh-KAI⟩ Chilli Urulai Kizhangu ⟨oo-ROO-lai kih-ZHUN-goo⟩ Potato Thengai ⟨THENG-guy⟩ Coconut Maampazham ⟨MAAM-puh-zhum⟩ Mango Yelumichai ⟨yeh-LOO-mih-chai⟩ Lemon Vellari-kai ⟨VEL-luh-ree-kai⟩ Cucumber Thakkali Tomato

docs.title: 5. Nature and Animals
docs.text: Tamil English Meaning Naay ⟨NAAY⟩ Dog Poonai ⟨POO-nai⟩ Cat Maadu ⟨MAA-doo⟩ Cow Paravai ⟨PAA-ruh-vai⟩ Bird Maram ⟨muh-RUM⟩ Tree Mazhai ⟨MUH-zhai⟩ Rain Veyil / Sooriyan ⟨VAY-yil / SOOR-ih-yun⟩ Sunlight / Sun Nila ⟨NIH-laa⟩ Moon Nadhi ⟨NUH-dhee⟩ River 📤 Share

docs.title: Adjust Maadi: Everyday Scenarios
docs.text:
Navigate everyday Bangalore logistics—from negotiating rent and dealing with BESCOM to bargaining with street vendors—all in Kannada. Put your social currency to the test!

🚀 Switch to larger screen view

[!TIP] Adjust Maadi is the official Bangalore philosophy. It means "please compromise" or "let's figure this out". Used correctly, it opens every door in the city!

docs.text:
Navigate Chennai streets, negotiate fares, give directions, and make friends — all in Tamil. Choose your words carefully!

[!TIP] Meter Podu means "Put the meter on" — an essential phrase in Chennai. Use it wisely!

docs.title: Suffix Station
docs.text:
Build Kannada words by clicking the right suffixes. Race the clock, score points, and master the art of agglutination!

🚀 Switch to larger screen view

[!TIP] Suffix Station tests your knowledge of Kannada's agglutination — how the language "glues" suffixes onto base words. Can you beat your high score?
